questions.description: Nico works in Associated Pills, a multinational firm that specializes in… Advise Nico if he has any claim in tort law…
questions.text: Nico works in Associated Pills, a multinational firm that specializes in producing a pharmaceutical pill to protect against the flu for export around the world. During the process of manufacture one of the raw materials must be heated to boiling point in specialised containers to ensure that any harmful micro-organisms are destroyed. Unfortunately, this means that Nico is forced to work where the temperature can often exceed 30 degrees Celsius, as he is required to continuously stir the containers in which the raw material is heated in. Nico is provided with gloves and an insulating suit to protect him from the heat but this has the practical impact of hindering his movement and slows down his work rate. He can work much quicker without the suit but it can become unbearably hot. Associated Pills mandates that the suit is worn at all times and that it is prohibited to enter the area without wearing both the suit and gloves. Nico however would usually wear the provided suit, despite the fact that the supervisors on the factory floor generally speaking did not tend to enforce this rule as other employees did not like wearing the suit. Recently, Associated Pills has decided to increase production, so they have put more containers in the same space until their new factory extension is complete. This is making movement in the area even more restricted. As a result Nico has taken to not wearing the suit provided at any stage. At the same time, his supervisor has become more active in the requirement that a suit be worn, and has told Nico several times that he should be wearing the suit. However, he has told them that in the new cramped conditions, the suit is more likely to cause an accident than protect him. One day, Nico collapses from heat exhaustion and is taken to hospital where he is told that he will not be able to work again as he has suffered irreversible damage from the hot conditions of his workplace. Advise Nico if he has any claim in tort law against Associated Pills.

questions.description: The use of damages in tort law is inappropriate in modern times in two ways.
questions.text: The use of damages in tort law is inappropriate in modern times in two ways. First, compensating victims through monetary payments is usually an inadequate form of redress and second, it often fails to act as a sufficient catalyst for change to prevent torts occurring in the first place. Critically discuss this statement.

questions.description: Tom and Harry have a long history of not liking each other, even though…
questions.text: Tom and Harry have a long history of not liking each other, even though they often frequented the same pub. One night they were out having a drink in the pub with their respective friends when the owner of the pub announced that he was running a competition on his Bucking Bull machine. This machine would simulate the situation where someone was sitting on top of a bull who was trying to throw the rider off. Whoever lasted the longest would win dinner for four. When Tom signed up, Harry also signed up saying 'I am not going to let that fecker win!' Each competitor had three rounds to see how long they would last. Tom and Harry were way ahead of everyone else by the end of the second round and so the competition was down to them alone. At that point, Harry decided that he desperately wanted to win, so after he had completed his turn, unknown to anyone, he turned up the speed on the Bucking Bull machine from Fast to Very Fast. His intention was solely to ensure Tom was thrown earlier than anyone else. When Tom got on the Bucking Bull, he was thrown violently from it, so violently that he landed outside the safety ring and fell on the hard floor, injuring his two legs. Suspecting that Harry had done something, Tom stood precariously in front of Harry and said 'You are not leaving here tonight until you admit what you did!' Harry replied 'Out of my way!' and brushed passed Tom heading towards the exit. Tom pushed Harry from behind who fell on top of Sarah who was carrying two pints back to her friend's table. As Sarah fell the pint glasses shattered injuring Sarah. Critically discuss the potential liability of Tom and Harry, if any, in tort law only.

questions.description: Sam owns a small derelict house which he is in the process of renovating to live in.
questions.text: Sam owns a small derelict house which he is in the process of renovating to live in. As he is doing the work by himself, it is taking a long time. Although he has the site boarded up, people in the neighbourhood have found ways to get into the derelict house and often use it to hang out for drinking parties. Sam tries his best to stop this from happening but he feels it is a losing battle. One night, Mary and Ann are sneaking some drinks in the derelict house when a beam falls on Ann and pins her to the ground, knocking her unconscious. Mary is scared that she will be in trouble if she is caught, so she decided to leave Ann and anonymously call the Guards saying someone was inside the house, believing that they would get an ambulance. Although the Guards call to the house they can hear nothing and presume it is a prank call. However, several hours later, Sam, on his way home from a late night drinking session with some friends, decides to check in on the house. He finds Ann, who is now semi-conscious. Sam picks Ann up and puts her into his car and drives her to the hospital. On the way however, because Sam is over the alcohol limit, they are involved in a collision with another car. Eventually Ann arrives at the hospital. She is found to have suffered long term injuries from exposure which could have been avoided if she had gotten to the hospital earlier. In addition those injuries have been exacerbated by her being moved from the house without the proper equipment and also from the accident that she was in with Sam. Critically discuss who is the legal cause of Ann's injuries from that night.

questions.description: Aileen owns an apartment in a large complex. Company.
questions.text: Aileen owns an apartment in a large complex. A couple of years ago, the owners of the complex built a small shopping centre with an attached multi-storey car park next to her apartment. The multi-storey car park is of an open plan design. Aileen had no objections to the development in the beginning but soon after it opened, she found that the noise from the cars parking during the day was quite annoying. Not only was there the sound of the engines, but also the squeal of the tyres and brakes, the honking of the horns, the smell of the exhaust fumes and the general noise as people left and returned to their vehicles. One day, one of the parked vehicles caught fire but it was thankfully put out in time before any major damage was done, although the smell in Aileen's apartment that time was horrendous. However what has really upset Aileen has been the decision of the company to offer overnight parking. This has resulted in a constant stream of cars coming and going throughout the night and not just during commercial hours. In the beginning the car park had 24/7 security but due to the recession, this has been reduced so that there is no security in place from midnight to 7am. As a result of this, some of the local teenagers are running through the car park in the middle of the night, pounding on the vehicles and setting of the alarms on the cars. They think it is hilarious but it keeps Aileen awake all night long. Aileen has lost patience and wants to close the car park down. Advise Aileen as to what action in tort, if any, she might have in these circumstances.

questions.description: It is often said that the liability of an employer for the acts of their… Vicarious.
questions.text: It is often said that the liability of an employer for the acts of their employee must be within the scope of their employment, or more traditionally, there will be no liability if the employee is on a 'frolic of their own.' But the imprecision that this brings renders the application of vicarious liability fraught with difficulty. Critically discuss this statement with respect to relevant jurisprudence in the law of tort.

questions.description: Harrison is a famous director of action movies. Director.
questions.text: Harrison is a famous director of action movies. He is currently working on Blood Robbing, a heist film where a bank is robbed and a chase ensues between the police and the criminals. Harrison is renowned for his gritty reality in filmmaking and, in particular, limiting his use of famous actors and opting to use as many 'real people' in his films as he can, as well as using unscripted elements to get a real reaction from the actors. While working on this film, he has hired a real bank located in a small suburb of a large city. The bank has agreed to vacate the property and he has rebranded it overnight using a fictitious name. Filming inside the bank is taking place by way of the security cameras to give it that authentic feel. He has assembled local residents for walk-on parts in the movie. Everything is set and the scene begins. Inside the bank, Sally, one of the 'robbers' suddenly pulls on a mask and draws a shotgun, pointing it in the air. The gun is fired twice as the 'robber' shouts 'Get down you scumbags -- unless you want to die, you pigs!' Suddenly one of the customers begins screaming uncontrollably. Unsure whether this is part of the unscripted element of the scene, Sally plays along pointing the gun at the screaming man, saying 'That's it, you are done for!' At this point Sally realizes that her 'victim' is not an actor as he has begun getting sick. In fact one of her 'victims' is Fred, a local resident who stumbled in on the scene and believed it was real. Since the event Fred has not been able to sleep and now cannot bring himself to go into any bank. Advise Fred as to what action in tort law, if any, he might have with respect to these facts.

questions.description: Critically discuss the difference between contributory negligence and… Negligenc.
questions.text: Critically discuss the difference between contributory negligence and assumption of risk in tort law with reference to relevant jurisprudence.

questions.description: The traditional view that intentional torts do not require harm is…
questions.text: The traditional view that intentional torts do not require harm is inconsistent with the primary policy goal in tort law of compensating victims of wrongful actions. If the plaintiff has suffered no harm, there is no purpose to a tort action. Critically discuss this statement in the context of the law on intentional torts and their usefulness for plaintiffs.

questions.description: Jane was a junior surgeon who had shown great promise. Consideration.
questions.text: Jane was a junior surgeon who had shown great promise. In this hospital, she was allowed to operate on her own without supervision, provided that the actual surgery was not so complicated that only a consultant surgeon would undertake the surgery in which case a junior surgeon could only observe. Unusually, this hospital had a practice that any decision was a judgement call by the junior surgeon based on the complexity of the operation and their personal skill and experience. One day Jane was undertaking a routine operation on her patient, Jim. During the course of that surgery, she unexpectedly came across a cancerous growth. After some consideration, Jane decided that she would take the opportunity to remove the growth. Although it was very close to a vital organ, Jane felt confident that she could handle it. Many surgeons would believe that Jane was wrong about that as the operation would be considered too complex for a junior surgeon. The theatre nurse was surprised because in other hospitals where she had worked previously, a junior surgeon would never conduct a procedure that they had not done before unless supervised by a consultant although this was not a standard requirement at this hospital. Nonetheless, the theatre nurse asked Jane if she should call for a consultant surgeon to take over but Jane said that it was not necessary. Jane told her that it needed to be done now and they could not wait until a consultant surgeon was available. Medically however, the majority of surgeons would disagree that it required immediate intervention. Jane successfully finished the operation, and all appeared well. However, several days later Jim was readmitted to hospital in agony: an infection had arisen where the cancerous growth had been removed. Advise Jim if Jane has breached the standard of care that she owed him while undertaking the operation.

questions.description: Dermot, a student, rented a house from Acme Homes under a short-term lease. Advise Pam and Jim if they have any action…
questions.text: Dermot, a student, rented a house from Acme Homes under a short-term lease. One of the terms of the lease was that no more than 6 people could be present in the house at any one time. The reason for this was twofold: one, to prevent noisy parties that might annoy the neighbours and two, the internal stairs was a circular steel staircase built around a pole which was rated for a maximum weight of 400kg or 6 people. Dermot was sick and tired of the government's pandemic restrictions which limited his social life considerably. He missed the night life and socialisation with his friends. Dermot decided to invite 10 of his friends to a small party and he bought in alcohol and food for a long night. On the night in question however, each of the people he invited also brought at least two other people with them. Dermot was getting worried that the party might attract attention from the police. Dermot then told everyone who was not invited to leave. However, the gate crashers became aggressive and refused to leave. Later that night, there was a competition to see how many people could get on the circular stairs. As the eighth person got on, the staircase collapsed, injuring two people who were not on the stairs: Pam, one of Dermot's guests and Pam's friend Jim, who had not been invited and had refused to leave when asked. Advise Pam and Jim if they have any action in tort law against Dermot.

questions.description: Amy operated a trampoline alley, which was simply a series of trampolines… Advise Gina as to who may be the cause of…
questions.text: Amy operated a trampoline alley, which was simply a series of trampolines set out within a large warehouse. People either individually or as part of a group would then pay to spend up to 30 minutes on the trampolines where they could exercise in fun. The trampolines had to be re-tensioned every morning before opening to ensure that they would provide sufficient 'bounce' to the participants. Amy was normally very careful about this, making sure not to provide either insufficient or excessive tension, but one morning she had been out late and as a result she over-tightened one of the trampolines, resulting in excess tension and increased 'bounce'. Terry was a bodybuilder who liked every now and then to have some fun exercise and so that morning, he had booked a trampoline. Shortly after he began, he noticed that there was significant 'bounce' which he really enjoyed although he did feel it was getting risky jumping so high. Unfortunately, on his last bounce he went so high that when he came down he landed on the trampoline next to his which was being used by Gina. As a result, Gina fell off her trampoline and landed on the ground and was knocked unconscious. Terry was so distressed that despite Amy telling him not to move Gina and wait for the ambulance, he picked Gina up and drove her to the hospital in his car. All the movement caused Gina's lung to be punctured from the broken rib she had suffered when she landed on the ground. Advise Gina as to who may be the cause of her injuries in a tort action.

questions.description: Joe owned a disused petrol station which he decided that he wanted to… Advise Mary if she has any action in tort…
questions.text: Joe owned a disused petrol station which he decided that he wanted to redevelop for apartments. Buried in the ground were steel tanks which were used to store petrol when the station was in operation. The tanks were designed to last for over 100 years and were buried some 10 metres underground. When the petrol station was closed, the bulk of the petrol stored in these tanks was sucked out and taken away. However, it was always realised that notwithstanding this, there would remain a small but not insignificant amount of petroleum residue which would remain in the tanks. Joe was advised that it would be sufficient to concrete over the tanks and this would render them absolutely safe. After this was done and as Joe was building the apartments, the local authority was widening the road leading to his development. In order to do this, they had to blast some rocks that were obstructing the completion of the road. In a very controlled explosion, they blasted their way through the rocks and the road was completed. Some months later, Mary, a farmer whose land was adjacent to Joe's property noticed the water from her [water] well had a foul oil-based smell and also contained other petroleum contaminants. An investigation concluded that the blast had caused damage to the buried tanks on Joe's development and the contents of the tanks were now leaking into the water table. Advise Mary if she has any action in tort against Joe in these circumstances.

questions.description: Critically discuss the liability of volunteers in the law of tort, both…
questions.text: Critically discuss the liability of volunteers in the law of tort, both under legislation and case law.

questions.description: Michael was a salesperson for CarsRus, a car dealership. Advise CarsRus as to their liability in tort, if any.
questions.text: Michael was a salesperson for CarsRus, a car dealership. He was working one Friday evening by himself when at around 4pm Angela dropped in and said that she was looking for a family minivan at a reasonable price. Michael had several vehicles to show her. Angela really liked one of the minivans that Michael had offered her and wanted to take it for a test drive. However, Angela did not have her driving licence with her and Michael was conscious that due to the pandemic, only one person was allowed in a vehicle at any one time. As a result of this, it was a strict policy of the dealership that no one could take a vehicle for a test drive without leaving a valid driving license. Angela seemed inclined to buy the car but was annoyed that Michael would not let her take it for a test drive and made that annoyance clear to Michael. As Michael was trying to explain to her that his hands were tied on this issue, Angela's husband, Dwight, arrived at the dealership in a panic. He explained that their own car had broken down and that he would now miss his flight because he would not be able to get to the airport in time. Angela now also became very anxious and asked Michael if he could take them to the airport. Michael was hesitant but was also fearful of losing a sale, so he reluctantly agreed. When they went outside, he was surprised to see Angela's two children also waiting. Undeterred Angela insisted that he take all of them to the airport and then demanded that they take the minivan she was interested in and that Angela could use the opportunity to test drive the vehicle. Again, Michael was reluctant, but the logic of Angela's request was compelling. On the way to the airport, while Angela was driving, she injured a pedestrian, Kevin. Advise CarsRus as to their liability in tort, if any.

questions.description: Critically discuss the development of product liability paying particular…
questions.text: Critically discuss the development of product liability paying particular attention to the introduction of the Liability for Defective Products Act 1991 and relevant case law.
